card.intuition: Store only one pointer per node — the XOR of the previous and next node addresses — so you can derive either neighbor given the one you came from.
card.analogy: A trail of breadcrumbs where each crumb encodes both the one before it and after it in a single mark. As long as you remember where you came from, you can always find where to go next.
card.time_complexity: O(1) for insert at ends; O(n) traversal
card.space_complexity: O(n) — halves pointer storage vs a standard doubly linked list
when_to_use: Doubly linked list functionality with only one pointer per node — halves pointer overhead in memory-constrained environments
Embedded systems or low-level memory management where pointer size matters
Understanding bitwise operations applied to memory addresses
gotchas: Not supported in garbage-collected languages (Python, Java, Go) — only in languages where you control raw memory addresses (C, C++, Rust unsafe)
You must always carry the 'previous node' pointer as you traverse — the npx field alone is useless without context
Any external reference to a node must hold it alive (no GC) — XOR of unmanaged pointers is undefined behavior if either is freed

exercise.prompt: Simulate XOR linked list traversal using integer IDs as proxy for memory addresses.
exercise.starter_code:
# We simulate with integer node IDs instead of real pointers.
# Each node stores npx = prev_id XOR next_id.
# 0 is the null sentinel.

def build_xor_list(values):
    """Return (nodes_dict, head_id) where nodes_dict maps id -> {val, npx}."""
    nodes = {}
    prev_id = 0
    first_id = None
    for i, v in enumerate(values, start=1):
        next_id = i + 1 if i < len(values) else 0
        npx = prev_id ^ next_id
        nodes[i] = {'val': v, 'npx': npx}
        if first_id is None:
            first_id = i
        prev_id = i
    return nodes, first_id

def traverse_xor_list(nodes, head_id):
    """Return list of values by traversing the XOR linked list forward."""
    result = []
    prev_id, cur_id = 0, head_id
    while cur_id != 0:
        result.append(nodes[cur_id]['val'])
        next_id = ___________      # derive next from npx XOR prev
        prev_id, cur_id = cur_id, next_id
    return result
hints: npx stores prev XOR next. You know prev_id, so: next_id = npx XOR prev_id
XOR is its own inverse: if npx = prev XOR next, then npx XOR prev = next
exercise.test_code:
nodes, head = build_xor_list([10, 20, 30, 40, 50])
assert traverse_xor_list(nodes, head) == [10,20,30,40,50], 'Forward traversal'
nodes2, head2 = build_xor_list([1])
assert traverse_xor_list(nodes2, head2) == [1], 'Single node'
nodes3, head3 = build_xor_list([7, 8])
assert traverse_xor_list(nodes3, head3) == [7,8], 'Two nodes'
print('PASS')
